name: Topology & Geometry
description: General topology, algebraic topology (homology, homotopy), differential geometry, Riemannian geometry. The mathematics of shape, space, continuity without coordinates, and intrinsic curvature. Homology groups and homotopy groups are powerful algebraic invariants for classifying spaces. Differential geometry is the language of general relativity. Depends on 444 (algebra via groups/homology) and 500 (calculus via smooth manifolds) and 533 (analysis via analytic structures). Feeds into 566 (linear algebra via tensors and fiber bundles).
axioms: Topological space axioms: open sets are closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections
Hausdorff separation axiom: distinct points have disjoint neighborhoods
Brouwer fixed-point theorem: every continuous map f: Dⁿ → Dⁿ has a fixed point
Fundamental group π₁: homotopy classes of loops based at a point form a group
Euler characteristic χ = V - E + F is a homotopy invariant
Gauss-Bonnet theorem: ∫_M K dA = 2πχ(M) — total curvature = topology
Stokes' theorem: ∫_∂Ω ω = ∫_Ω dω — the fundamental theorem of differential forms
reasoning_patterns: invariant computation (Euler characteristic, Betti numbers, fundamental group)
manifold detection and dimension estimation in data
cohomology: dual of homology, richer algebraic structure
fiber bundle classification (vector bundles, principal bundles)
boundary_conditions: High-dimensional topology (≥ dimension 4) is fundamentally different — exotic R⁴ exists
Classification of 3- and 4-manifolds is incomplete or very difficult
Topological data analysis is sensitive to metric choice in practice
The Poincaré conjecture (proved by Perelman) was open for a century
